requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must maximize 401(k) employee salary deferral contributions to reduce current taxable income while building tax-deferred retirement savings through employer-sponsored retirement plan
criteria: Must be eligible employee in employer's 401(k) plan meeting age and service requirements
2025 employee deferral limit: $23,500 for participants under age 50
Contributions can be traditional (pre-tax) reducing taxable income or Roth (after-tax) for tax-free growth
Elective deferrals made through payroll deduction as percentage of compensation or fixed dollar amount
Can change contribution rate during year subject to plan rules and elections
Consider front-loading contributions early in year to maximize tax-deferred growth period
Coordinate with employer match to ensure capturing full matching contribution opportunity

401k_employee_contribution_rules.deferral_limit_2025: $23,500 under age 50; $31,000 age 50+ with catch-up contribution
401k_employee_contribution_rules.tax_deduction: Traditional 401(k) contributions reduce taxable wages and current income tax
401k_employee_contribution_rules.roth_option: Roth 401(k) contributions taxed currently but provide tax-free qualified distributions
401k_employee_contribution_rules.immediate_savings: Traditional contributions save taxes at marginal rate (10-37%) plus 3.8% NIIT if applicable
401k_employee_contribution_rules.employer_match: Employer match (if any) doesn't count toward employee deferral limit; separate limit applies
401k_employee_contribution_rules.contribution_timing: Front-loading contributions maximizes time in market for investment growth

restrictions: Highly compensated employees ($155,000+ in 2024) may face additional restrictions under ADP testing
Total plan contributions (employee + employer) cannot exceed $70,000 (2025) or 100% compensation
Cannot withdraw funds before age 59½ without 10% penalty except for specific hardship or separation events
Required minimum distributions begin at age 73 (age 75 if born 1960 or later)
Loans from 401(k) permitted if plan allows but create repayment obligations and potential tax consequences
Job change: can roll over to new employer 401(k) or IRA; avoid taxable distribution
Roth 401(k) subject to RMDs during owner's lifetime unlike Roth IRA
State tax treatment varies; a few states don't provide deduction for pre-tax contributions
